summary: O'Neil shares a personal story about her youngest son's recovery from a broken leg, where the physical healing was quick but the psychological barrier to walking again proved far more challenging. After weeks of frustration, conflicting medical advice, and a misdiagnosis of 'hysterical paralysis,' she finally gave up the intense pressure and took her son on a relaxing swimming trip. Once the stress was removed, he simply decided to walk again. The experience taught her that sometimes the best parenting means stepping back and trusting children to overcome obstacles on their own timeline.
key_points: Medical professionals offered conflicting and sometimes unhelpful advice, including an expensive psychiatric referral for 'hysterical paralysis'
Physical therapy provided some progress but the breakthrough came when pressure was removed entirely
The pool allowed her son to feel 'fully able-bodied' again, rebuilding his confidence without the stress of expectations
The hardest parenting lesson is learning when to stop trying to fix things and simply trust your child
money_quotes: It's been a blizzard of pancakes and lost teeth, and almost nothing has really fazed me.
The guy told me he had 'hysterical paralysis' and gave me the number of a psychiatrist who charges $1500 per hour and doesn't take insurance.
I could see how joyous he became in the water, where there was no sense of gravity and he was once again fully able-bodied.
I think he would have slept in it if I'd let him.
On the way out of the elevator and on to the check-out desk, my son said to me, 'I'm just going to walk now.' And he did.
The hardest part has been learning to trust my kids to get through difficult things even when I can't help them directly.
key_insight: Sometimes the most effective parenting intervention is removing pressure and trusting children to find their own path through difficult challenges.
